figures.title: Signature-based identification of volatility models from path geometry
figures.caption: Figure 5.1: Confusion matrix for Experiment 5.1 (ntest = 50,000). — This confusion matrix displays the classification performance of a volatility model identification system tested on 50,000 samples. The matrix compares predicted labels (columns) against true labels (rows) across four volatility model classes: Heston, OU, rBD.1, and rBD.3. The diagonal elements show nearly perfect classification with counts of 50,000, 50,000, 49,999, and 49,998, indicating the model achieves extremely high accur

figures.title: Signature-based identification of volatility models from path geometry
figures.caption: Figure 5.2: Confusion matrix for Experiment 5.2 (ntest = 50,000). — This figure shows a confusion matrix displayed as a heatmap comparing true labels versus predicted labels for a classification model with four classes (rBD.15, rBD.4, rBD.6). The matrix has dimensions 4×4 with the diagonal elements (50000, 50000, 49992, 49985) representing correctly classified samples shown in dark blue, while off-diagonal elements show misclassifications. figures.title: Stable Sentiment and Persistent Dynamics in U.S. Economic News over 45 Years
figures.caption: Figure 1: Statistical characterization of the daily news sentiment index from 1980 to 2025. figures.title: Arbitrage-Free Multi-Maturity Risk-Neutral Marginals
figures.caption: Figure 1 reports ϵavg and ϵmax as the number of observed quotes increases. Even with only 10 observed quotes, ϵavg is already below 5 × 10−5, and by 20 quotes ϵmax is below 3 × 10−5. figures.title: Arbitrage-Free Multi-Maturity Risk-Neutral Marginals
figures.caption: Figure 2 gives a direct density-and-price comparison with the SSVI benchmark, and shows that the benchmark shape is already recovered accurately on a moderate quote grid and very closely by n = 50. Table 2 reports the per-fit runtime as a function of n at a single maturity. figures.title: Arbitrage-Free Multi-Maturity Risk-Neutral Marginals
figures.caption: Figure 1: Single-maturity SSVI benchmark accuracy. The figure reports the average relative price error ϵavg and the maximum relative price error ϵmax of the reconstructed call price as the number of observed quotes n increases. Errors are evaluated at 1000 off-grid moneyness points in [0.5, 1.5], excluding the observed quotes used as inputs. — This log-log scatter plot displays marginal fitting error under Stochastic Volatility Inspired (SSVI) model as a function of the number of observed quotes

figures.title: From Gravity to Confinement: Wealth Redistribution as Optimal Drift Design in the Fokker-Planck Framework
figures.caption: Figure 1: Convergence to target wealth distribution under different tax structures. The proportional tax (blue, solid) converges at the demographic turnover rate δ; the progres- sive tax (red, dashed) converges at the progressivity rate κ; a more strongly progressive tax (black, dotted) converges faster still. figures.title: Can Reinforcement Learning Efficiently Discover Price Manipulation?
figures.caption: Figure 1: The optimal long position, nα∗, as a function of δ for the PnD strategy. — This line graph plots the optimal long position (nα*) on the y-axis against the impact exponent (δ) on the x-axis for a pump-and-dump (PnD) strategy. The curve shows a monotonic decline from approximately 3.2 at δ=0.2 to near 0 at δ=0.9, illustrating that as market impact increases with higher exponent values, the optimal position size decreases substantially.
